facts_disclosed: Defender died, got a Toyota Hilux

turns.content: whats the best way to get mud off a hilux flatbed without scratching the liner. just got the thing and already treating it like its made of glass.

The good news is Hilux bed liners are pretty tough -- most are made of a textured polymer that's designed to take abuse. For mud:

1. **Rinse first** -- pressure washer or a hose on a strong setting. Get the bulk off before you touch it with anything.
2. **Soft bristle brush** with warm soapy water for anything stuck on. Avoid stiff brushes or scourers -- they won't damage the liner structurally but can dull the texture over time.
3. **Avoid solvents** -- just soap and water. Some people use diluted all-purpose cleaner (like Autoglym or similar) for stubborn mud but it's usually not necessary.
4. **Let it dry, then check** -- if there's a film left after drying, a quick wipe with a damp microfibre cloth sorts it.

Is this the factory liner or did you get an aftermarket one fitted?
factory one. its the invincible spec so it came with the liner already in. prob overthinking it tbh. just not used to having a vehicle thats actually new. my last one was 27 years old.

Ha, that's quite the jump -- 27 years old to brand new. I can see why you're being precious about it. The novelty of having a vehicle that doesn't need constant nursing will wear off eventually, but enjoy it while it lasts.

The Invincible liner is solid. It's a proper over-rail type, so it protects the top edges too. Honestly, these things are made to be used -- farm mud, tools, equipment, whatever. You'd have to really try to damage it.

Are you planning to use it for work? If so, a rubber mat on top of the liner is a cheap extra layer of protection and makes sliding heavy stuff in and out easier.

aye its my work truck. im a vet so its full of kit. drugs cabinet bolted in the back. calving ropes. the usual. the old defender had all that too but everything in it was covered in a layer of mud and cow muck and I think at some point I just stopped caring. now ive got a clean truck and I want to keep it that way for at least a month.

A month is an ambitious target for a farm vet's truck but I admire the optimism. A few things that'll help:

- **Rubber floor mats** for the cab if you haven't already -- the Invincible might come with them but check they're the deep-tray type, not the flat carpet ones.
- **A boot liner or tray** for the back if you're putting kit in the cab area too.
- **Seat covers** -- even basic ones will save the upholstery from the inevitable eau de farmyard.
- **Keep a pack of wipes or a microfibre cloth in the glovebox** for quick clean-ups between calls.

For the flatbed with the drugs cabinet and equipment, a rubber mat on the liner is definitely the way to go. Stops things sliding, easier to hose down, and protects the liner from bolt marks and scratches from metal kit.

What happened to the Defender in the end? 27 years is a good innings.

gearbox went. again. third time. first two times I got it fixed but this time the mechanic basically said the whole drivetrain was done and it would cost more than the truck was worth to sort it. which tbh was probably true about five years ago as well I just kept ignoring that.

That's the classic Defender death spiral -- each repair costs more than the last and at some point you're just keeping a museum piece on the road through sheer stubbornness. Third gearbox failure is a pretty clear sign the universe was telling you to move on.

The Hilux is the right call for a working vehicle. Toyota drivetrains are boringly reliable -- you'll probably miss having something to fix on weekends. People run Hiluxes for 200,000+ miles without major drivetrain issues.

Did you keep the Defender or sell it on? Some people buy them for the chassis and body even when the mechanicals are shot.

sold it to a lad who restores them. got more for it than I expected tbh. defenders hold their value even when theyre basically dead. he seemed genuinely excited about it which I found baffling but good luck to him.

The Defender market is mad -- even rusted-out shells go for decent money because the restoration community is huge and parts are interchangeable across decades. Sounds like he got a project and you got a contribution toward the Hilux, so everyone wins.

You'll probably go through a grieving period where the Hilux feels too smooth and too quiet and too easy. Then winter will hit, you'll start it first time in minus five, the heater will actually work, and you'll wonder why you didn't switch years ago.
the heater. aye. the defenders heater had two settings. off and slightly less cold. already had the hilux heater on this morning and it was actually warm within about 30 seconds. felt like cheating.

Welcome to the 21st century. Wait until you discover the Hilux has working windscreen demisting, a radio that picks up more than two stations, and doors that don't require a full-body shove to close.

Seriously though, for someone doing farm calls in a Highland winter, having a reliable vehicle with proper heating isn't a luxury, it's basic welfare. You'll be a better vet when you're not arriving at every call half-frozen and wondering if the truck will start again when you're done.

thats a fair point actually. used to sit in the defender for ten minutes before calls just to defrost my hands enough to examine an animal. the hilux I can just get out and get on with it. still feels wrong that nothing rattles though. too quiet. cheers for the cleaning tips anyway. ill enjoy the novelty while it lasts.

Give it time -- the silence is just reliability being polite. If the Hilux starts rattling, that's when you worry. With a Defender, rattling just meant it was Tuesday.

Keep the rubber mats in, hose the flatbed regularly, and accept that by January it'll have the same layer of farm muck as the old one. At least underneath the muck it'll still be working properly. Good luck keeping it clean -- in the Highlands, in November.
